data.title: Genesco tops Q4 revenue expectations on steady demand across segments
data.description:
Overview

  • Footwear company's Q4 revenue grew 7% yr/yr, beating analyst expectations

  • Adjusted operating income for Q4 beat analyst estimates

  • Company's strategic initiatives and Journeys brand drove positive results

Outlook

  • Genesco expects fiscal 2027 comparable sales to grow 1% to 2%

  • Genesco forecasts fiscal 2027 total sales to be flat to down 1%

  • Genesco projects fiscal 2027 adjusted EPS between $1.90 and $2.30

Result Drivers

  • JOURNEYS PERFORMANCE - Journeys led with double-digit comparable sales growth, driven by holiday performance and strategic initiatives

  • COST MANAGEMENT - Selling and administrative expenses decreased, reflecting cost savings initiatives

  • STRATEGIC INITIATIVES - Product elevation and customer experience strategies resonated with teens, driving market share gains

Analyst Coverage

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 2 "strong buy" or "buy", 2 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell"

  • The average consensus recommendation for the apparel & accessories retailers peer group is "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Genesco Inc is $38.00, about 45.6% above its March 5 closing price of $26.09

  • The stock recently traded at 12 times the next 12-month earnings vs. a P/E of 17 three months ago

data.title: Markets price in a more than 50% chance of one ECB rate hike by July
data.description:
Key points:
  • German 2-year yields set for biggest weekly rise in almost 3 years
  • Investors are awaiting remarks from ECB’s Isabel Schnabel later in the session
  • Strategists say it's too early to price additional rate hikes

By Stefano Rebaudo

Money markets increased their bets on further European Central Bank tightening on Friday, pricing in more than a 50% chance of a 25 basis‑point rate hike by the summer as the Middle East conflict stoked inflation fears.

German short-dated yields inched higher again and were set for their biggest weekly rise in almost three years.

Germany's 10-year government bond yield (DE10YT=RR), the bloc's benchmark, was flat at 2.85%, after reaching a fresh one-month high at 2.878%. It is on track for a 20-bp weekly rise, the biggest since March 2025.

Money markets are pricing in a 55% chance of a European Central Bank rate hike by July (EURESTECBM4X5=ICAP) and an 85% chance by December (EURESTECBM7X8=ICAP).

"I think it’s too soon to assume the ECB will hike again, oil prices are basically back at 2024 levels," said Massimiliano Maxia, senior strategist at Allianz Global Investors.

"If energy prices climb further, then the picture could change," he added.

Crude oil was headed on Friday for its sharpest weekly gain since Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022.

SCHNABEL COMMENTS IN FOCUS

"With (ECB board member) Isabel Schnabel scheduled to speak in New York late in today's session, market participants are probably nervous about a 'zero tolerance' message and wary not to take the other side in the hawkish ECB trade," said Christoph Rieger, head of rates and credit research at Commerzbank.

Germany's two-year yields (DE2YT=RR), which are more sensitive to policy expectations, were up 1.5 bps at 2.27%. They hit 2.31% earlier in the session, their highest since March 6.

"Traditionally, oil price shocks tend to be stagflationary for the euro zone, which often motivated the ECB to simply look through oil-driven inflation surges," said Carsten Brzeski, head of macro strategy at ING.

"However, the risk of such an approach is falling behind the curve, as could be witnessed in 2022," he added.

There is no "preset pace for our monetary policy stance," President Christine Lagarde said on Thursday. Bank of Spain governor Jose Luis Escriva said it was "very improbable", based on current information, that the bank would make a decision on interest rates in the governing council's next meeting.

"If our central scenario proves to be correct, however, the shock should be quite modest and short-lived," said Raphael Olszyna-Marzys, international economist at J. Safra Sarasin, after arguing that most estimates suggest that a 15% rise in oil prices would add about 0.2-0.4 percentage points to headline inflation over the coming year and trim GDP growth by 0.1-0.3%.

"Rate cuts may be delayed, but outright rate rises still look unlikely," he added.

Italy's 10-year government bond yields rose 1.5 bps to 3.59%. The gap versus Bunds stood at 73 bps. It hit 53.50 in mid-January, its lowest since August 2008.

data.title: Royal London profits jump 18% as AUM hits record high
data.description:
Group adjusted operating profit was 18% higher than 2024 at £327m, while AUM increased to a new peak of £199bn, from £173bn previously, helped by a £6bn contribution from the year's acquisition of Dalmore Capital. Net inflows were £4.1bn, compared with £1.0bn of net outflows.

Royal London's flagship Governed Range, where most of its pensions customers are invested, attracted net inflows of £2.6bn, with AUM reaching £83bn, up from £72bn previously.

Protection new business sales were up 17% at £991m, which the firm said was driven by a stronger proposition and increased market share.

Meanwhile, the bulk purchase annuity buy-business performed "particularly strongly" in its first full year of trading, with 18 completed transactions and £1.3bn of premiums.

“We recorded another strong performance in 2025 with operating profit up 18%, reflecting the positive momentum across our business," said chief executive Barry O'Dwyer.

"This was supported by our first full year in the bulk purchase annuities market, where we secured a series of key transactions as trustees and advisers valued the stability and long-term commitment that a mutual can offer."

data.title: Chile's annual inflation hits lowest level in over six years in February
data.description:
Chile's 12-month inflation fell in February to its lowest level in more than six years, data from statistics agency INE showed on Friday.

Annual inflation in the world's largest copper producer hit 2.4%, slowing from the 2.8% recorded the previous month, and registering its lowest rate since September 2019.

Consumer prices stood below the midpoint of the central bank's 2% to 4% target range, reinforcing rate-cut expectations after a prolonged hiatus.

Chile's central bank held borrowing costs steady at 4.5% in January, but traders expected the institution to cut its key rate by 25 basis points to 4.25% at its next meeting on March 24.

"Under normal conditions, this inflation profile would support further easing," Pantheon Macroeconomics' chief Latin America economist Andres Abadia said in a note, also warning that geopolitical risks and energy-price volatility have significantly increased policy uncertainty.

Traders expect the rate to remain at 4.25% for the next 12 months, after the expected cut.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices remained flat in February, while a Reuters poll of economists had predicted a 0.1% increase.

The monthly increase was mainly driven by clothing and footwear, along with food and non-alcoholic beverages costs, INE added.

data.title: Prada Faces Challenges This Year — Market Talk
data.description: Prada's brands face a number of challenges in 2026, including the necessary revamp of Versace, Citi analysts Thomas Chauvet and Alberto Cecchetto say in a note. The Italian luxury fashion company reported fourth-quarter sales and second-half EBIT broadly in line with expectations. "Despite positive current trading commentary, we still see limited visibility on the magnitude of any potential growth acceleration at the Prada brand this year," the analysts say. Furthermore, the group's Miu Miu label faces risks of growth slowing further, they add. For Versace, visibility remains low on both the scale and timing of the required turnaround, the analysts say. Citi maintains its neutral rating on the stock. Shares closed at 40.88 Hong Kong dollars. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

data.title: Kristi Noem Is Out. Will Trump Stop There? — WSJ
data.description:
By Damian Paletta

Good morning. Kristi Noem had a no good, very bad day on Capitol Hill Tuesday.

Thursday was worse.

That's when she was fired.

After Noem was publicly excoriated by two Senate Republicans earlier this week, President Trump began asking aides whom he should pick to replace his Department of Homeland Security secretary.

He found his man in Sen. Markwayne Mullin (R., Okla.). And on Thursday, Trump said Noem was out, and Mullin was in. Just like that.

Noem's problems have snowballed since last year, and she faced enormous criticism for referring to two protesters killed by U.S. agents in Minnesota earlier this year as "domestic" terrorists. Shortly after she was fired, the White House issued a vicious anonymous statement blaming her for all sorts of mishaps. It could be read as a warning to other cabinet officials that if they are shown the door, it won't be pretty.

During Trump's first term, he was constantly firing cabinet officials. This term has — so far — been relatively calm. Noem was particularly scandal-plagued. Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer has similarly fallen under a lot of scrutiny lately. Trump has privately expressed displeasure with Attorney General Pam Bondi, though that seems to have eased.

Presidents often will shake up their team after the midterm election to bring in fresh faces and move out folks who might be obvious targets of political investigations. Will Trump wait until November, or is this just the beginning?

This is an edition of the Politics newsletter, bringing you an expert guide to what's driving D.C. every day. If you're not subscribed, sign up here.

People and Policies I'm Watching

War in the Middle East: Follow live updates on the conflict as Israel said it is moving from the "surprise strike phase" to the "next phase" of its campaign against Iran.

Jobs report: The February number is scheduled for release at 8:30 a.m. ET.

Trump's Friday: The president is scheduled to meet with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth at 2:30 p.m., and then with Interior Secretary Doug Burgum at 3:30 p.m. Trump will participate in a college-football roundtable at 4 p.m. and later depart the White House for Miami.

What I'm Following

Trump chose a staunch ally to replace Noem. Sen. Markwayne Mullin is a former MMA fighter who has spent years as a "Trump whisperer" — talking to the president, by his own account, "all the time." During talks last year between GOP lawmakers and the White House regarding Trump's tax cuts, Mullin helped negotiate between different factions fighting over the cap on state and local tax deductions.

Hegseth is finding his voice as the defender of the attacks on Iran. The defense secretary had a bruising confirmation process when he was pilloried over his personal problems and lack of experience running a major organization. Now the typically press-averse Hegseth has appeared repeatedly at the Pentagon briefing lectern to forcefully defend Trump from accusations he is dragging the U.S. into the type of foreign conflict he said he would avoid.

The new playbook for U.S.-led regime change is 'We bomb, you decide.' In Iran and in Venezuela, Trump ordered military action to decapitate national leadership, then said it was up to the people to take the next steps if they wanted to. The goal is to extract tangible wins for the U.S. without the risk of deploying large ground forces.

What Else Is Happening

  • Rep. Tony Gonzales (R., Texas) said he wouldn't seek re-election, hours after top Republican House leaders asked him to drop his re-election bid.
  • Pentagon officials are drawing up plans to replenish U.S. munitions expended fighting Iran over the past week.
  • Interventions overseas have dominated the White House's recent focus -- and cast fresh uncertainty on a fragile economy.
  • Anthropic Chief Executive Dario Amodei said the company will challenge in court its designation by the Defense Department as a security threat and supply-chain risk.
  • Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. just found out how to start a revolt in Boston: diss Dunkin' Donuts.

What I'm Reading

  • Trump Says Mullin Will Replace Noem as DHS Secretary (The Oklahoman)
  • Trump Despises RINOs. So Why Is He Reportedly About to Endorse One of the Swamp's Biggest? (The Federalist)
  • Newsom Looks Small in the Face of a Big War. (The Free Press)

This Week in Washington History

In 1946, Winston Churchill, ousted as British prime minister, traveled with President Harry S. Truman to Westminster College in Fulton, Mo., and delivered his era-defining speech in which he warned that the U.S. and the U.K. needed to be prepared to confront an expansionist Soviet Union. "From Stettin in the Baltic to Trieste in the Adriatic, an iron curtain has descended across the Continent," he famously said. "Behind that line lie all the capitals of the ancient states of Central and Eastern Europe."

About Me

I'm Damian Paletta, The Wall Street Journal's Washington coverage chief. I've covered Washington for 22 years as a reporter and editor. I've covered the White House, Congress, national security, the federal budget, economics and multiple market meltdowns.

WSJ Politics brings you an expert guide to what's driving D.C., every weekday morning. Send your feedback to politics@wsj.com (if you're reading this in your inbox, you can just hit reply). This edition was curated and edited in collaboration with Joe Haberstroh and Alina Heineke. Got a tip for us? Here's how to submit.

data.title: Indonesia sentences two Thai nationals for smuggling nearly two tons of methamphetamine
data.description:
By Yudi Saputra

An Indonesian court on Friday sentenced one Thai national to life in prison and another to 17 years for smuggling nearly two tons of methamphetamine, in one of the country's biggest drug busts in years.

Here are some details about the verdicts:

  • Weerapat Phongwan was sentenced to life in prison for acting as an intermediary and overseeing drug distribution, a judge said.

  • Teerapong Lekpradub got 17 years in jail after being found guilty of acting as an intermediary in the sale and purchase of the drugs, the judge said.

  • Both defendants and four Indonesians were arrested by Indonesian police in May last year after the fishing boat they were aboard was found to be carrying 67 boxes of methamphetamine, weighing nearly 2 tons, court documents showed.

  • The drugs were wrapped in tea bags, footage of a police expose showed.

  • They were arrested in the waters of Karimun, Riau Islands province, near Singapore.

  • The same court on Thursday sentenced another defendant Fandi Ramadhan to five years in jail.

  • The court will issue a verdict for the other three defendants next week.

  • The case went viral on social media after Ramadhan's family asked President Prabowo Subianto to intervene and free him.

  • The drugs were smuggled from Phuket, Thailand and believed to be sourced from an international network operating in Thailand, Myanmar, and Laos, local media reported.

  • Indonesia has strict drug laws and in 2015 it executed two Australians in the Bali Nine drug ring for attempting to smuggle heroin out of the resort island of Bali.
